name: Arctic Seasonal Round
description: The seasonal cycle of the Inuit — shaped by sea ice, Arctic light, and the rhythms of marine and terrestrial mammals. In the Arctic, the distinction between 'seasons' is not four but many: the Inuit recognize detailed transitional periods tied to ice conditions, animal behavior, and the sun's position above or below the horizon.

seasons.name: Siku (Sea Ice) Season / Dark Period
ecologicalMarkers: Sea ice forms and thickens — becomes safe for travel at 10-15 cm (walking), 30+ cm (snowmobile/qamutiik)
Polar night — in the high Arctic, the sun does not rise above the horizon
Ringed seals (natsiq) maintain breathing holes (aglu) through the ice
Arctic fox (tiriganiaq) active and trappable
activities: Aglu hunting — lying in wait at seal breathing holes using a rod to detect the seal
Qallunaat (snow house / iglu) construction at hunting camps
Qulliq (seal-oil lamp) construction and maintenance — the lamp is the source of heat and light in the iglu
Dog team (qimmit) travel on sea ice
Arctic fox trapping
Qamutiik (sled) construction and repair
seasons.culturalContext: The dark period is paradoxically a season of intense activity for seal hunters. Moving to sea ice camps, constructing igluit (plural of iglu), and hunting seals at their breathing holes in darkness and extreme cold required mastery of all Arctic survival skills. The qulliq was not just a lamp — it was the technology that made habitation of the Arctic possible.

seasons.name: Ukiuq (Deep Winter) / Solstice Period
ecologicalMarkers: Coldest temperatures of the year
Sea ice at maximum thickness and extent
Polar bear (nanuq) dens on land — females with cubs
Sun begins to return above the horizon in late January
activities: Continued seal hunting at aglu (breathing holes)
Polar bear hunting using dog teams (traditional method now largely ceremonial)
Kamik (sealskin boot) construction and repair — wet boots in Arctic conditions are life-threatening
Sinew sewing — watertight seams for outer garments
Amauti (women's parka) construction and repair
Storytelling and throat singing (katajjaq) in the iglu during long nights
Building snow windbreaks and improving iglu insulation
seasons.culturalContext: Deep winter is the season of the iglu, the qulliq, and the community gathered around the lamp. Throat singing (katajjaq) was traditionally a women's practice, performed in pairs, as entertainment during long winter confinement. It is Level 1-2 — publicly demonstrated and shared, but cultural context is required.

seasons.name: Upirngaksaaq (Early Spring) / Return of the Sun
ecologicalMarkers: Sun returns above the horizon — 24-hour darkness ends
Sea ice still thick and solid — best travel conditions
Ringed seals begin hauling out to bask on ice
Bearded seals (ugjuk) appear at ice edges
activities: Maupok hunting — still-hunting basking seals on sea ice (requires extreme patience and camouflage movement)
Qajaq launching after winter storage — sealskin skin must be rehydrated and checked
Longer qamutiik (sled) trips made possible by good light and firm ice
Walrus (aiviq) hunting at floe edges
Harp seal hunting during migration (Nunatsiavut, Nunavik)
Harvesting marine plants and invertebrates exposed at low tide
seasons.culturalContext: Spring brings the return of light and the season's best sea-ice travel conditions before break-up. This is the peak season for qajaq use and open-water hunting. The qajaq is perhaps the pinnacle achievement of Arctic material culture — a individually-fitted craft that is an extension of the hunter's body.

seasons.name: Upirngaaq (Spring) / Break-Up
ecologicalMarkers: Sea ice begins to deteriorate and break up
Arctic char begin upstream migration
Eider ducks, Arctic terns, and snow geese arrive
Muskox calves born on the tundra
activities: Sea mammal hunting from qajaq and umiak at floe edges before break-up makes ice travel dangerous
Beluga whale (qilalugaq) hunting — community drive hunts using umiaq
Waterfowl hunting — eider duck and geese
Char fishing at river mouths as fish begin migration
Gathering of edible coastal plants — sea purslane, Labrador tea
Muktuk (beluga skin and blubber) preparation and preservation
seasons.culturalContext: The beluga whale drive hunt is one of the most community-intensive heritage skills in Inuit culture, requiring the coordination of multiple umiaq and families working together — a direct expression of Piliriqatigiinniq (IQ-06: working together for a common purpose). Muktuk is both a food and a critical source of Vitamin C in the traditional Arctic diet.

seasons.name: Aujaq (Summer) / Open Water
ecologicalMarkers: Open water — sea ice absent
Midnight sun in the high Arctic
Caribou accessible on tundra
Arctic char at peak activity
activities: Caribou hunting on the tundra — driving herds into water for qajaq-based hunting
Caribou hide preparation — critical timing as summer hides are thinner and best for clothing
Arctic char fishing at weirs (stone fish traps built across river shallows)
Narwhal hunting — Nunavut, Nunavik
Gathering crowberries, cloudberries, and sea vegetables
Umiak travel along coastlines — summer travel mode
Drying meat and fish on racks (pipsi — caribou jerky)
seasons.culturalContext: Summer caribou hunting provided a second major source of food and the most important source of clothing material. Caribou hides taken in July and August have short hair and are ideal for inner garments. Hides taken in fall have longer hair for outer garments. This seasonal precision in material gathering reflects deep ecological knowledge.

seasons.name: Ukiaksaaq (Early Winter Freeze-Up)
ecologicalMarkers: Temperatures dropping below freezing consistently
Sea and lakes beginning to freeze
Caribou herds moving toward winter range
First snows arrive
activities: Final caribou hunting of the season
Food cache preparation — storing preserved meat and fish under rocks for winter
Iglu construction training and practice as conditions allow
Dog team preparation — checking harnesses, repairing qamutiik
Moving from summer tent camps to more substantial winter shelters
Gathering materials for winter craft work — sinew, skins, antler, bone
seasons.culturalContext: Freeze-up is the transition period between open water and solid-ice seasons. It can be the most dangerous time of year for travel — ice is forming but not yet safe. The knowledge of when ice is thick enough for travel was literally life-or-death knowledge, passed from experienced hunters to younger generations through direct mentorship (Pilimmaksarniq).
